# Transcrição: 05-How to Use Manual Bidding to Leverage Results

## [00:00 - 03:03]

Alright, so what I'm going to talk to you guys about in this video is your bidding strategy. So keep in mind that the Facebook ad platform is an auction, so your bidding against your competitors to get real estate in front of your target audience. So there's lots and lots of millions of advertisers out there and some of them are going after the exact same people that you are and Facebook is the one who gets to decide whose ads are put in front of this audience based on three really important factors. So the first factor is your ad account quality score. Now no one really knows how Facebook calculates this and you can't really see the score, but things that come into play are aspects like how old is the ad account, how much money has gone through it, do you keep trying to get ads across that keep getting banned or denied and are you doing any dodgy things in there. Those types of factors will all determine the quality score of your ad account. The second thing that comes into consideration is the engagement score. So how likely is this target audience to engage with the ad that you've created. So oftentimes Facebook feels like, yeah it's not really very likely so it's going to store your campaigns. So sometimes that's why new campaigns kind of get fed out a little bit and then they die a slow death. So that can be because Facebook has deemed that the engagement score is really low. And then the third thing is your bidding strategy. So how much are you willing to spend for an action on your Facebook ad compared to your competitors. So what we're going to look at now is the bidding strategy and I'm going to take you into the ad set level over here and if we scroll down to the bottom underneath this optimization and delivery is where we'll find the bidding strategy section. So I'm just going to jump into this one as an account over here. So Facebook will automatically set your bidding strategy to automatic funnily enough. So when you run campaigns this will automatically be set to auto and we recommend that you keep it set there at least until your campaign optimizes after a few days. So when you first launch a campaign just run with the automatic feature on and let Facebook do its thing it's going to try and get you the best result possible. Now we only really review the bidding strategy if you're not getting the results that you're after or if for example your campaigns are stalled. Then one of the things that you can do is control your bidding amount. So when you're first running your campaigns leave it set to auto but if your campaign's not working out then you can switch it to manual over here. Now when it comes to manual bid Facebook's been changing things up a lot lately so there's a few different things that you might see. So firstly they've gotten rid of the suggested bid range for starters that used to show up down here so I'll show you in a little bit how to work out what kind of range you might be able to expect. But first off let's take a look at what should be there when you select manual. So the first thing to note is that what you're bidding on will change depending

## [03:03 - 06:05]

on what objective you're using. So you can see right here we're in a traffic campaign so we're bidding on clicks but if we come in here and we change this to conversion we're now bidding on conversions and if we change that to a reach campaign we're now bidding on what a thousand impressions is worth. So we'll jump back into that traffic campaign and even within this you might notice that there's some differences. So for example this ad account over here is showing us lowest cost or set a bid cap whereas this one over here is also in a traffic campaign but we've got automatic and manual bid options. Essentially these are different ways of doing the same thing so the lowest cost is essentially auto bid and that's just going to get us the best price possible for our budget versus this one over here it'd be the same for automatic bid and then for manual bid it's us saying to Facebook a click is worth three dollars or it's worth one fifty or whatever that the case is. When it comes to conversion campaigns it's a little bit different as well so for example over here if we switched to manual bid we've now got two options we've got on manual bid we can either set it as a maximum bid or an average bid so essentially what you're telling Facebook is I don't want to pay more than seven dollars per conversion no matter what I don't want any of the conversions to cost me more than that versus average manual bid of seven dollars you're saying I want to I want my conversions to average out to that amount so I don't care if one costs me ten dollars and then another one costs me four dollars as long as the average cost per conversion is around seven dollars I'm happy with that so this maximum bid over here is essentially the same thing on this side if we set a bid cap on the lowest cost so this was um let's actually change this to conversion quickly and you'll see here now we have different options we've got lowest cost with this with a bid cap set or we've got target cost which is essentially the average bid on this one so those are the two options there now in terms of what kind of bid to expect as I mentioned Facebook used to give us a really handy bid range in terms of what the average bids might be or what other advertisers are paying and you can kind of use that to benchmark what your manual bid should be or what you can expect to pay so since they've taken that away one way that you can sort of look at that is we'll just change that to a hundred so that it's a more manageable figure so right now we don't have any targeting so let's just change this to uh do I have any saved

## [06:05 - 08:15]

audiences here yes perfect so this is set to New Zealand males age 28 to 41 who are interested in motorcycles automobiles or cars and it's saying for a hundred dollar budget we can expect to get 84 to 510 clicks per day so if we pull up a calculator so a hundred dollars divided by 510 would be a cost per click of 19 cents or a hundred dollars divided by 84 is a dollar 19 so we can expect to pay anywhere between 19 cents to a dollar 19 per click so that's a really rough idea of the the bid range that we can expect so just keep in mind that that's Facebook's estimated daily results so you're not guaranteed to get 84 to 100 sorry 84 to 510 clicks it's just estimated based on your campaign being fully optimized or your ad set being fully optimized and performing properly that you can expect that range so it is a really rough estimate so if you want to go into the middle of that you could add on your 19 cents that did not work go one dollar 19 plus point 19 roughly 69 cents a click so that would be quite a cheap click if you wanted to ensure that you're beating out your competition you'd obviously go a lot higher than not just the average but probably the the highest cost per click that you could expect and then you'll make sure that you're really hitting your target market fully and it's the same thing for conversions so right now I believe this is a conversion campaign it's not giving us any estimated daily results because this one doesn't have any active conversions no still not showing so if you had that same range for your conversions over here then you'd be able to use that create that same calculation and get an idea
